title: Cargo ships observed near Strait of Hormuz as Iran categorizes vessels into hostile, neutral, and friendly
summary: Maritime intelligence reports show cargo vessels queuing near the Strait of Hormuz as Iran implements a three-tier categorization system for transit — hostile, neutral, and friendly — while the US threatens sanctions on ships paying Iranian transit fees.
contents:
## Maritime Intelligence: Vessel Classification System at Strait of Hormuz

### Iranian Vessel Categorization
Iran has implemented a three-tier classification system for vessels seeking Strait of Hormuz transit, dividing all states into "hostile," "neutral," and "friendly" categories, according to Iranian state television reporting. Ships from countries categorized as hostile face the strictest restrictions and highest risk of interdiction.

### Vessel Observations
Maritime tracking shows cargo ships queuing near the strait as commercial operators attempt to navigate the competing US and Iranian restrictions. The US naval blockade prevents Iranian tankers from exiting while Iran's own restrictions impede allied-nation commercial traffic.

### Sanctions Crossfire
Vessel operators face a dilemma: paying Iranian fees to transit Hormuz now risks US sanctions, while refusing Iranian requirements risks interdiction or denial of passage by Iranian forces. The competing frameworks have effectively paralyzed normal commercial traffic through the waterway that normally handles 20% of the world's traded oil.

### AIS Analysis
Tracking data reveals abnormal vessel movement patterns in the Gulf of Oman and approaches to the strait, with many commercial operators choosing longer routes around the Cape of Good Hope rather than risking Hormuz transit. Insurance premiums for Hormuz passage have reached unprecedented levels.

**Source:** Reuters maritime, IRNA, National Post
